title-group.article-title: Enhancing IR‐MALDESI MSI Spatial Resolution Through Beam Constriction With a Ring‐Actuated Iris

aff.named-content: Biological Imaging Laboratory for Disease and Exposure Research (BILDER), Department of Chemistry

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Rapid Communications in Mass Spectrometry published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Rationale
sec.p: Infrared matrix‐assisted laser desorption electrospray ionization (IR‐MALDESI) mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) enables label‐free, spatially resolved molecular analysis of biological tissues under ambient conditions. While improving spatial resolution is important for mapping fine tissue structures, mid‐infrared (IR) lasers are diffraction limited to laser spot sizes greater than ~5 μm, necessitating creative approaches to enhance spatial detail for subcellular MSI.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: An adjustable, ring‐actuated iris combined with a reflective objective was integrated into the IR‐MALDESI MSI platform, replacing the conventional beam expander and collimator assembly. Laser beam diameter on target was systematically varied, and ion abundances were measured using high‐resolution mass spectrometry. Normalized ion abundances were fitted to a linear model as a function of beam diameter to predict signal at increasing spatial resolutions.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Ion abundance decreased linearly with decreasing laser beam diameter across the tested range. Linear regression of normalized ion abundances enabled prediction of signal levels at progressively higher spatial resolutions. The tunable aperture provided stable beam control and reproducible ablation, allowing quantitative assessment of sensitivity trade‐offs associated with reduced laser spot size.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Controlled beam restriction using an adjustable iris enables fine tuning of spatial resolution in IR‐MALDESI MSI by deliberately discarding a large fraction of high laser power while maintaining measurable ion abundance. Far‐field diffraction and tissue ablation thresholds confine material removal beyond simple geometric scaling, and linear modeling of ion abundance versus beam diameter enables quantitative prediction of signal loss at high spatial resolution.

institution-wrap.institution: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) has emerged as an effective technique for spatially resolved molecular analysis, offering insights into the distribution of a variety of biomolecules directly from tissues. Among various MSI modalities, infrared matrix‐assisted laser desorption electrospray ionization (IR‐MALDESI) has gained prominence due to its ability to analyze hydrated or frozen tissues under near‐native conditions with little to no sample preparation [1]. By combining mid‐infrared (IR) laser desorption with orthogonal electrospray post‐ionization, IR‐MALDESI enables ablation of neutral molecules while maintaining molecular integrity and minimizing fragmentation [2, 3, 4, 5]. As with most laser‐based imaging modalities, the achievable spatial resolution is inherently limited by the wavelength, causing unique challenges in IR‐MALDESI, as the mid‐IR laser is longer than the more commonly used ultraviolet (UV) lasers [4, 6].

One of the ongoing challenges in MSI is the balance between spatial resolution and ion abundance. High spatial resolution is crucial for accurately mapping molecular distributions at the cellular or subcellular level, resolving microanatomical features such as tissue boundaries, vascular structures, and heterogeneous cell populations [7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12]. Such resolution is particularly important for studies of disease progression, tumor microenvironments, or localized metabolic changes, where subtle chemical profiles can provide key biological insights [13, 14, 15]. Matrix‐assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI) MSI routinely achieves spatial resolutions below 10 μm and as low as 1 μm due to the use of short‐wavelength UV lasers [16, 17, 18, 19]. While laser‐based techniques like MALDI are widely utilized, other ionization modalities, including DESI and nano‐DESI, play significant roles in biological MSI. These techniques achieve spatial resolutions on the order of approximately 20–100 μm for DESI [20] and as low as 7 μm for nano‐DESI [21] under optimized conditions. Alternative geometries, such as transmission mode MSI, can enable higher spatial resolution by allowing small focal length lenses to be placed very close to the sample of interest without blocking the path towards the mass spectrometer. Transmission mode MSI has demonstrated subcellular resolution, achieving a pixel size of 600 nm to precisely localize lipids in the mouse brain and kidney [22, 23].

Mid‐IR lasers are commonly utilized in MSI because they can selectively excite the O‐H stretching bands in water, eliminating the need for external matrices or vacuum conditions [24, 25]. However, their longer wavelength results in a larger diffraction‐limited spot size and broader energy deposition, making it more difficult to ablate microscale regions of the tissue [18]. As a result, achieving 10‐μm spatial resolution with an IR laser often requires specialized optical designs or beam‐shaping strategies. Infrared matrix‐assisted laser desorption/ionization (IR‐MALDI) has achieved 30‐μm spatial resolution through optimization of beam focusing [26], and other IR laser ablation systems have demonstrated sub‐50‐μm spatial resolution by extending the laser beam path to overfill the focus lens, increasing fluence at the sample surface [27]. Within IR‐MALDESI, spatial resolution has previously been improved to 50 μm using a Schwarzschild reflective objective [28], as well as an adjustable iris [29]. In this current study, we combine these optical approaches. In addition to optical modifications, spatial resolution improvements have been achieved through alternative acquisition strategies, such as oversampling, which has enabled effective pixel sizes approaching approximately 10 μm for IR‐based systems [6] and about 6 μm for UV‐based systems [30, 31]. Oversampling improves apparent spatial resolution by stepping the sample stage in smaller increments than the beam diameter, but does not reduce ablation diameter, leading to nonuniform sampling and limiting true spatial resolution. Oversampling also complicates data analysis as each laser spot does not contain the same volume of ablated tissue, making it difficult to compare relative abundances. Separately, the use of a mid‐IR laser with improved beam quality, with a M2 of 4, recently enabled 20‐μm resolution in IR‐MALDESI [32], demonstrating the additional advantages possible when advanced laser sources are employed.

Building upon these developments, we explore a complementary strategy for improving spatial resolution by using a high‐power mid‐IR laser and discarding most of its energy through an adjustable iris to modulate beam diameter prior to focusing. Using this approach, we demonstrate high spatial resolution MSI of mouse brain tissue at 25 μm, enabling detailed visualization of regional molecular heterogeneity while maintaining sufficient ion abundance for robust annotation. This configuration allows controlled reduction of effective spot size by limiting the divergent spatial modes from the diffraction limit, producing a quantifiable relationship between iris diameter, ablation characteristics, and ion abundance. Rather than defining absolute performance limits, these experiments quantify how desorbed mass, ion abundance, and annotation counts scale with beam restriction. By fitting normalized ion abundances to a linear model across iris diameters (5–2.5‐mm and 67–23‐μm spot sizes), we establish a framework that describes the rate of signal loss with increasing spatial resolution. This approach provides practical guidance for balancing sensitivity and resolution in IR‐MALDESI and offers a simple means to adjust the excitation beam diameter over the area of interest to fine‐tune imaging performance.

sec.title: Materials
sec.p: LC–MS grade water, acetonitrile (ACN), methanol (MeOH), and formic acid for the electrospray solvent composition were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Nazareth, PA). Nitrogen gas was purchased from Arc3 gases (Raleigh, NC). One millimeter microscope glass slides (plain) and coverslips were purchased from Fisher Scientific (Pittsburgh, PA). Liver tissue was stained with Arcturus HistoGene Staining Solution and Permount, both of which were purchased from Fisher Scientific. Burn paper for qualitative beam diameter measurements was purchased from ZAP‐IT (Barrington, NJ).

sec.title: Sample Information
sec.p: Mouse liver and brain tissue were used for iris optimization experiments. Samples were obtained from the Ghashghaei Laboratory at North Carolina State University (NCSU), Department of Molecular Biomedical Sciences, where animals were maintained in accordance with the NCSU Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) guidelines. Following sacrifice, the tissues were harvested, flash‐frozen on dry ice, and stored at −80°C until sectioning. Liver (7 μm thick) and brain (10 μm thick) sections were cut using a Leica CM1950 cryostat (Buffalo Grove, IL) at −15°C and thaw‐mounted onto plain glass microscope slides (1 mm).

sec.title: Laser Optical Train Setup
sec.p: In the present work, we aim to further enhance spatial resolution by simplifying the optical setup, specifically, by removing the beam expander and collimator and instead incorporating a graduated ring‐actuated iris (ThorLabs, Newton, NJ, SM1D12C) positioned directly before a Schwarzschild reflective objective (ThorLabs, Newton, NJ, LMM15X‐P01). The system used a burst‐mode laser (JGMA Associates, Burlington, MA, USA) at a wavelength of 2.97 μm, delivering 7‐ns pulses with an energy of 500 μJ per pulse at a 10‐kHz repetition rate within each burst, and a burst frequency of 50 Hz. The optical apparatus is shown in Figure 1A. The adjustable iris allows precise control of the beam diameter in millimeter‐scale increments (Figure 1B), enabling dynamic tuning of the laser spot size at the sample surface. During experiments, the iris aperture was varied to select the desired beam diameter, which was then delivered to the sample through the reflective objective, without requiring additional optical alignment steps. Corresponding laser fluence at the sample surface varied as a function of iris diameter and was calculated based on measured beam diameters on target. All other laser parameters (pulse energy, repetition rate, and focusing geometry) were held constant unless otherwise noted.

caption.p: (A) Schematic of the conventional, high spatial resolution, and higher spatial resolution IR‐MALDESI optical configurations in which the beam expander and collimator are removed and replaced with a ring‐actuated iris (SM1D12C) positioned before a Schwarzschild‐like reflective objective (LMM15X‐P01). The adjustable iris enables fine control of beam diameter in millimeter increments, allowing systematic tuning of the laser spot size. (B) Larger iris openings (e.g., 5 mm) produce larger ablation spot sizes, resulting in lower spatial resolution. Conversely, smaller iris openings (e.g., 3 mm) yield a more tightly focused beam, enabling smaller spot sizes and higher spatial resolution.

sec.title: IR‐MALDESI MSI
sec.p: All MSI experiments were conducted using the Next Generation IR‐MALDESI source coupled to an Orbitrap Exploris 240 mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Bremen, Germany) [33]. A 2.97‐μm mid‐IR laser (JGM Associates Inc., Burlington, MA) with a Gaussian beam profile (M2 ≈ 5) was employed to desorb neutral species from regions of interest (ROI) across the tissue surface. The laser operated at a repetition rate of 10 kHz and delivered energy in bursts of up to 10 pulses per burst. Neutral molecules were subsequently post‐ionized via an orthogonal electrospray plume composed of 60:40 (v/v) acetonitrile/water with 0.2% formic acid. The electrospray was maintained at a flow rate between 1 and 2 μL/min with a 3–4‐kV potential applied for stable ionization.

Mass spectra were acquired in positive ion mode over an m/z range of 100–1000 at a resolving power of 240 000 (FWHM at m/z 200) in native centroid mode. Internal calibration was enabled using the EASY‐IC source with fluoranthene ([M•+], m/z 202.0777) as the reference ion, ensuring a mass measurement accuracy (MMA) within ±2.5 ppm. To synchronize ablation events with spectral acquisition and minimize the accumulation of ambient ions, automatic gain control (AGC) was disabled, and the injection time was fixed at 15 ms [34]. Prior to tissue analysis, the laser was focused on burn paper mounted on a glass slide to verify alignment for each optical configuration.

sec.title: Data Analysis
sec.p: Tissue sections were optically imaged and measured immediately after analysis with a Leica LMD 7000 microscope (Leica Microsystems, Buffalo Grove, IL). XCalibur (Thermo Fisher Scientific) was used to analyze raw mass spectra. For imaging analysis, .RAW files were converted to .mzML files with MSConvert [35] then to .imzML files with imzMLConverter [36]. MSiReader [37, 38] v3.10 (MSI Software Solutions LLC, Raleigh, NC) was used for ion abundance extraction. Annotations were generated by uploading the converted .imzML files to METASPACE and annotating using the LIPIDMAPS database with an m/z tolerance of 3 ppm and a false discovery rate (FDR) of 10%. All provided annotations were manually compared against background signals to ensure each ion was specifically observed on‐tissue, rather than only in the ambient environment.

sec.title: Results and Discussion
sec.p: To evaluate how beam restriction influences ablation characteristics and spatial resolution in IR‐MALDESI, a series of experiments were performed using adjustable iris diameters to modulate the beam power and size prior to focusing. These adjustments affect the beam waist and fluence distribution at the sample surface. By systematically varying the iris diameter, we aimed to determine the smallest achievable spot size capable of complete tissue ablation while maintaining sufficient sensitivity for imaging applications. As shown in Figure 2A, reducing the iris diameter from 5 to 2.5 mm systematically decreased the average ablation spot size from approximately 67 to 23 μm, as measured by optical microscopy. These results highlight that ablation size can be modulated through changes in iris diameter. The corresponding laser fluence for each iris diameter decreases monotonically with reduced aperture size due to the combined effects of beam restriction and reduced pulse energy. Laser fluence is defined as the energy delivered per unit area at the sample surface (J/cm2), representing the spatial energy density of the laser. In this work, fluence was calculated as the energy per pulse divided by the effective beam area at the focal plane (πr 2) on a per‐pulse basis assuming the burst energy (0.5 mJ) is distributed across 10 pulses. Corresponding pulse energies (J) and effective beam areas (cm2) for each iris‐defined spot size are provided to enable quantitative comparison of irradiation conditions. Because laser fluence is defined as the energy delivered per unit area at the sample surface, concentrating the laser energy into a smaller spot size increases the local energy density even when the total pulse energy is reduced. The adjustable iris acts as a circular aperture which diffracts the low beam quality laser pulse (M2 > 5) far away from the focusing objective to improve the focusing of the optical system. In combination with the reduced power on target, this confines material removal to a smaller ablation area. The relationship between iris diameter and ablation spot size is approximately linear within this range, demonstrating that finer laser focusing can be achieved without major modifications to the optical components.

caption.p: (A) Average ablation spot size and respective laser fluences as a function of iris diameter observed on mouse liver tissue, demonstrating that reducing the iris from 5 to 2.5 mm decreases the spot size from ~67 to ~23 μm. Laser fluence values are reported on a per‐burst basis and were calculated from the measured spot diameters assuming a circular beam profile, with a 0.5‐mJ burst energy distributed across 10 pulses (fluence = energy per pulse/beam area [πr 2]). Measurements were obtained via Leica optical microscopy. (B) Representative ablation spots at 3‐ and 2.5‐mm iris diameters showing that at 2.5 mm, there is not full ablation of the tissue. (C) Effect of step size on sampling at a fixed 3‐mm iris diameter. Step sizes of 30, 35, and 40 μm were tested; 35 μm provides sufficient ablation overlap without oversampling, 30 μm introduces partial overlap, and 40 μm leaves unsampled regions.

sec.p: A closer examination in Figure 2B compares ablation at 3‐ and 2.5‐mm iris diameters. While the 2.5‐mm setting produced the smallest observed ablation spots, it did not fully ablate through the tissue, reflecting diffraction losses from the iris and lowering of fluence at beam focus. With higher pulse energy and a different beam profile, complete ablation at comparable spot sizes should be achievable despite diffraction losses. In contrast, the 3‐mm iris produced consistently well‐defined craters (~32–36 μm) that fully penetrated the tissue, indicating an optimal balance between resolution and ablation efficiency. To assess the ideal step size at the lowest achievable iris diameter, Figure 2C demonstrates the effect of step size at a fixed 3 mm. Stage step sizes of 30, 35, and 40 μm were evaluated to assess overlap and sampling density. The 35‐μm step size provided sufficient ablation overlap without oversampling, while the 30‐μm step introduced partial overlap and the 40‐μm step left unsampled regions between ablation spots. These results identify practical operating parameters for achieving high spatial resolution in IR‐MALDESI under the current laser configuration, while maintaining consistent ablation and sampling efficiency. We note here that these parameters are specific to the laser used but provide a simple, general procedure for optimizing IR laser performance to improve spatial resolution.

This approach is distinct from conventional focusing strategies, typically used for MSI. Rather than optimizing spot size through lens adjustment or beam expansion, the high energy output of the laser is partially blocked by the iris, effectively discarding a large portion of the beam's energy. This unconventional use of a high‐power laser highlights a simple yet powerful means of achieving better spatial resolution using lasers with low beam quality (M2 > 2), which are more typical in the IR wavelength range.

The diffraction‐based method used improves spatial resolution by reducing the power on target, allowing for a smaller sampling size for the same focal spot, and producing a far‐field diffraction pattern, which can be focused more tightly than the same system without the iris. This behavior can be understood in terms of Gaussian beam propagation and diffraction, where the iris modifies both the effective beam waist and angular divergence of the beam. This can be demonstrated using Fraunhofer diffraction modeling in LightPipes 2.1.5, of a perfect Gaussian beam (M2 = 1), a Gaussian beam shape with random phase, and a circular beam with random intensity to represent a system with poor beam quantity, as shown in Figure 3.

caption.p: (A) Comparison of laser root‐mean‐square (RMS) spot size as a function of iris size. The symbol w0 represents the beam diameter at the iris position. (B) Comparison of power transmission as a function of iris size. The perfect Gaussian beam and Gaussian beam with random phase variation overlap.

sec.p: We model the effect of far‐field diffraction of an iris on beam focusing with an initial beam width of 2 mm. We consider an ideal Gaussian beam and two degraded cases in which random noise is added either to the phase profile (phase distortion) or to the intensity profile (speckle or multimode effects). These perturbations are used to simulate poor beam quality systems (lasers with high M2 values). For Figure 3A, the RMS laser spot size is plotted as a ratio to compare the beam spot size with and without the iris. For apertures 1.5× larger than the 1/e2 radius of the incoming beam (ω0), the beam is largely unperturbed and all three modeled cases (ideal Gaussian, random phase, and random intensity beams) converge to a spot size ratio of 1.

As the iris is reduced to the range of 0.7–1.2 ω0, the focal spot decreases because the aperture transmits the high‐intensity central part of the beam and blocks distorted or low‐intensity outer regions and diffracts the incoming beam. With this, the iris acts as a spatial filter, preferentially transmitting the central Gaussian portion of the beam while suppressing distorted or divergent components. The reduction is strongest for an ideal Gaussian beam (red), and weakest for the random phase beam (blue) due to its larger angular spread induced by random phase. For very small apertures (< 0.5 ω0), the spot size ratio increases as diffraction from the aperture dominates, becoming the limiting factor, increasing the beam divergence and broadening the focal spot size. Figure 3B shows the power transmission associated with the iris closing. The power increases monotonically with iris radius and saturates near unity once the iris is larger than the beam. The perfect Gaussian and random phase beams have similar transmission curves, while the random intensity beam (green) loses more power for a given iris diameter because more of its energy is in the outer wing of the beam.

For the IR‐MALDESI laser with a high M2 value, both phase and intensity distortion exist. Within this framework, the iris simultaneously acts as a spatial filter (removing low intensity or distorted beam components) and a diffraction aperture (introducing angular broadening as the aperture approaches or becoming smaller than ω0). Combining both plots together, we can see that diffraction and aperture induced power truncation through the iris both contribute to the manipulation of laser spot size. Picking an iris diameter around or slightly larger than the diameter (FWHM) of the laser beam improves the focused spot size after the lens, avoiding diffraction broadening.

In this work, spatial resolution and the number of molecular annotations are shown to be controlled by iris diameter through its effective laser spot size and resulting tissue ablation volume. Figure 4 illustrates the relationship between spatial resolution and the number of molecular annotations obtained in IR‐MALDESI MSI experiments as a function of spot size. Each stacked column represents the distribution of major lipid classes, fatty acids (FA) and conjugates, glycerolipids (GL), glycerophospholipids (GP), sphingolipids (SP), sterol lipids (ST), prenol lipids (PR), and polyketides (PK), detected at a given iris diameter. A total of 115, 113, 75, and 53 lipid annotations were identified at increasing spatial resolutions, demonstrating a steady decline in molecular coverage as the iris was constricted. Over half of the lipid annotations observed at the lowest spatial resolution were lost at the highest resolution, indicating the strong impact of beam diameter on overall ion abundance and possible competition effects, described below.

caption.p: Relationship between spot size, spatial resolution, and molecular annotations in IR‐MALDESI MSI. Each stacked column shows the distribution of major lipid classes (FA, GL, GP, SP, ST, PR, and PK) at different iris diameters. A total of 115, 113, 75, and 53 annotations were detected at 67‐, 51‐, 34‐, and 23‐μm spatial resolution, respectively. Ten equivalent on‐tissue spots were used per spatial resolution condition for annotation comparison.

sec.p: As the iris diameter decreased, producing smaller laser spots, ion abundances across all lipid classes are reduced. Beyond confirming the expected decrease in signal with smaller ablation volumes, our results quantify this relationship, showing how ion abundance scales with effective spot size under a controlled beam restriction approach. Larger iris diameters produce higher ion signals and capture a broader range of lipid species, while smaller diameters increase spatial detail but reduce the number of detectable annotations. While the relative proportions of lipid classes remain broadly consistent, all classes show reduced representation at smaller iris diameters, reflecting a general loss of sensitivity. In other words, this effect is linear, where reducing the spot size decreases the sampled volume, causing ion abundances for some species to fall below signal‐to‐noise limits.

As the beam diameter decreases, fewer analytes are ejected into the electrospray plume, reducing the total number of molecules available for ionization. By fitting the data from the four iris settings to a linear model of the form y = mx + b, where x represents spot size and y represents normalized ion abundance, we obtained the slope and y‐intercept to estimate theoretical ion abundances at intermediate spatial resolution, using the previously described laser parameters. Normalized ion abundances were calculated as the absolute ion abundance measured at each spot size divided by the maximum abundance observed across those four conditions, such that normalized abundance = I/Imax. In most cases, the maximum value corresponded to the 67‐μm spatial resolution, which evaluates to 1 in the normalized plots.

The linear model provides a theoretical estimate of ion abundance as a function of spot size and is not constrained by detector sensitivity. As a result, the model predicts nonzero abundances even at spot sizes where experimental detection reaches zero, so measured cutoffs are dictated by limited analyte availability and instrument sensitivity rather than fundamental desorption or ionization efficiency. This approach allows quantification of the rate of signal loss with increasing spatial resolution. In Figure 5A, four representative lipids were chosen to illustrate the overall trend observed across most detected species: Ion abundance decreases as spatial resolution increases. The theoretical line hits zero only as spatial resolution nears the submicron range, whereas the experimental data reach zero abundance around 20 μm. This reflects detection limits rather than true molecular absence. At very small beam diameters, the desorbed plume contains fewer neutral molecules overall, reducing the total number of analytes that can reach the electrospray region. While a smaller number of neutrals could, in theory, lessen competition for charge and slightly improve ionization efficiency, the absolute number of ions generated still decreases because of the limited material available for post‐ionization. The lower plume density also reduces overlap with the electrospray, and the smaller ablation volume may lead to incomplete desorption. These effects cause the measured abundance to drop to zero more rapidly than predicted by the ideal theoretical model, emphasizing that spatial resolution improvements are ultimately constrained by both desorption volume and total analyte availability rather than ionization inefficiency alone.

caption.p: Normalized ion abundances of selected lipids as a function of spatial resolution in IR‐MALDESI MSI of mouse liver tissue. (A) Four representative lipids are shown from the majority class of detected ions (80 annotations; 70%), which display decreasing relative ion abundance with increasing spatial resolution, consistent with reduced ablation volume and ion yield. (B) A smaller subset of analytes (10 annotations; 8%) exhibits increased abundance at higher resolution, likely due to reduced ion suppression and more efficient ionization under lower total ion flux. (C) The remaining ions (25 annotations; 22%) are relatively constant across resolutions, suggesting ionization efficiencies that are less affected by ablation volume or plume density.

sec.p: As expected, reducing the iris diameter generally decreased signal intensity due to ablation of a smaller tissue volume. Interestingly, some lipids showed increased relative abundance at smaller iris diameters (Figure 5B). This may reflect reduced ionization competition in the electrospray plume, where lower‐abundance species experience less suppression and ionize more efficiently. At smaller spot sizes, fewer high‐abundance or easily ionized species are present to dominate the electrospray process, allowing otherwise suppressed lipids to ionize more efficiently. These results point to possible ionization competition effects, where reduced co‐desorption of dominant species favors the detection of less abundant lipids [39, 40]. Local tissue heterogeneity may also contribute, as smaller ablation volumes can preferentially sample regions enriched in specific metabolites. Figure 5C suggests balanced opposing effects where some lipid classes reflect constant ion abundances across all spatial resolutions. Similar to the previous observation, these particular lipids may maintain efficient ionization due to favorable chemical properties or uniform tissue composition. The lack of a strong dependence on spatial resolution indicates that their ionization dynamics are less sensitive to changes in ablation volume or plume density compared to other lipid types.

All annotated species observed at the smallest iris diameter condition (2.5 mm) were also detected and annotated at the larger iris diameter condition (5 mm). No additional species were uniquely observed at either condition, indicating that the primary effect of increasing spatial resolution under the conditions used here is a reduction in ion abundance rather than a change in qualitative molecular coverage. These results emphasize the complexity of how spatial resolution influences both desorption efficiency and downstream ionization behavior in IR‐MALDESI. While most species show the expected loss in ion abundance at higher resolution, deviations from this trend highlight the role of chemical composition, competition effects, and microenvironmental factors in ion abundance.

Figure 6A presents representative ion images acquired at 25‐μm spatial resolution, highlighting the spatial localization of selected lipid species across a mouse brain tissue section. The lipid species studied include FA 18:2 (m/z 279.2330), FA 18:1 (m/z 281.2486), and FA 22:6 (m/z 327.2330), along with phosphatidylserine PS 40:6 (m/z 834.5291). Collectively, these species exhibit broad and partially overlapping distributions across tissue, with no distinct localization to discrete anatomical regions. However, subtle differences in their spatial abundance patterns contribute to a contrast that helps distinguish overall tissue structure. Despite reduced signal associated with smaller ablation volume at higher spatial resolution, the data demonstrate that major anatomical features remain discernible. The increased spatial resolution at 25 μm improves the visualization of fine structural heterogeneity within the brain tissue that may otherwise be undetectable at lower spatial resolutions. When compared with the corresponding optical image (Figure 6B) and the annotated tissue atlas (Figure 6C) [41, 42], the ion images show general agreement with known structural regions, including broad cortical and subcortical areas. The spatial patterns enhance the ability to differentiate anatomical features and resolve tissue heterogeneity at higher resolution.

caption.p: (A) Representative ion images of selected lipid species at 25‐μm spatial resolution acquired from a mouse brain tissue section. (B) Corresponding optical image of the same tissue section used for imaging with (C) annotated mouse brain anatomy. Labels are cerebral cortex (CTX), corpus collosum (cc), ventricles (VL), caudate putamen (CP), anterior commissure (aco), and hypothalamus (HY).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This work introduces a novel strategy for tuning spatial resolution in IR‐MALDESI MSI through controlled beam restriction. By deliberately discarding a large fraction of a high‐power laser via an adjustable iris, the most intense portion of the Gaussian beam is delivered to the tissue, enabling fine control over effective spot size while maintaining measurable ion abundance. Additionally, far‐field diffraction from the iris reshapes the focal fluence distribution. In combination with the ablation threshold of the tissue, material removal is confined to a smaller area than would be expected from the total energy of the laser. Combining this approach with linear modeling of ion abundance versus beam diameter provides a quantitative framework for predicting signal loss at smaller spot sizes. The observed effect does not follow simple geometric effects alone (i.e., the differences in volume ablated) but instead reflects molecule‐dependent efficiency and well‐documented nonlinear and competitive ion generation processes. This concept emphasizes how optical energy distribution can be leveraged to balance structural detail and sensitivity, offering a flexible and applicable method for optimizing IR‐MALDESI performance under diverse experimental conditions.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Sarah M. Ashbacher: formal analysis, visualization, writing – review and editing, writing – original draft. Seth M. Eisenberg: formal analysis, visualization, writing – review and editing, writing – original draft, methodology. Alexander A. C. Wainwright: formal analysis, visualization, writing – review and editing, methodology, conceptualization. Aosheng Gu: formal analysis, visualization, writing – review and editing, methodology, conceptualization. Syeda N. Mahdia: software, formal analysis. R. J. Dwayne Miller: supervision, methodology, conceptualization. David C. Muddiman: conceptualization, investigation, funding acquisition, writing – review and editing, supervision, resources.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: David C. Muddiman is a partial‐owner of MSI Software Solutions, LLC. R.J. Dwayne Miller holds a patent related to Pulsed IR ablation for mass spectrometry.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Institutes of Health (R01GM087964) and the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (RJDM). All mass spectrometry measurements were conducted at the Molecular Education, Technology and Research Innovation Center (METRIC) at NC State University.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author.

ref.mixed-citation: M. Caleb Bagley , K. P. Garrard , and D. C. Muddiman , “The Development and Application of Matrix Assisted Laser Desorption Electrospray Ionization: The Teenage Years,” Mass Spectrometry Reviews 42, no. 1 (2023): 35–66, 10.1002/mas.21696.34028071 PMC11609267

J. A. Barry and D. C. Muddiman , “Global Optimization of the Infrared Matrix‐Assisted Laser Desorption Electrospray Ionization (IR MALDESI) Source for Mass Spectrometry Using Statistical Design of Experiments,” Rapid Communications in Mass Spectrometry 25, no. 23 (2011): 3527–3536, 10.1002/rcm.5262.22095501 PMC3781580

A. Tu and D. C. Muddiman , “Internal Energy Deposition in Infrared Matrix‐Assisted Laser Desorption Electrospray Ionization With and Without the Use of Ice as a Matrix,” Journal of the American Society for Mass Spectrometry 30, no. 11 (2019): 2380–2391, 10.1007/s13361-019-02323-2.31502226 PMC6937789

G. Robichaud , J. A. Barry , and D. C. Muddiman , “IR‐MALDESI Mass Spectrometry Imaging of Biological Tissue Sections Using Ice as a Matrix,” Journal of the American Society for Mass Spectrometry 25, no. 3 (2014): 319–328, 10.1007/s13361-013-0787-6.24385399 PMC3950934

P. Nemes and A. Vertes , “Laser Ablation Electrospray Ionization for Atmospheric Pressure, In Vivo, and Imaging Mass Spectrometry,” Analytical Chemistry 79, no. 21 (2007): 8098–8106, 10.1021/ac071181r.17900146

M. Nazari and D. C. Muddiman , “Cellular‐Level Mass Spectrometry Imaging Using Infrared Matrix‐Assisted Laser Desorption Electrospray Ionization (IR‐MALDESI) by Oversampling,” Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry 407, no. 8 (2015): 2265–2271, 10.1007/s00216-014-8376-5.25486925 PMC4359048

L. Zhang and A. Vertes , “Single‐Cell Mass Spectrometry Approaches to Explore Cellular Heterogeneity,” Angewandte Chemie, International Edition 57, no. 17 (2018): 4466–4477, 10.1002/anie.201709719.29218763
